Here is a simple model of a pension fund. You know you will need to pay out=
 a bunch of money=C2=A030 years from now, so you buy some 30-year governmen=
t bonds and hold them to maturity. When the bonds mature in 30 years, you h=
ave money, which you give to the pensioners, and you=E2=80=99re done. This =
model is obviously oversimplified,
  [1]
 but it=E2=80=99s a good start.
Let me make three points about this model. First, a financial point: Doing =
a pension fund this way is expensive. Thirty-year UK gilts (government bond=
s) paid about 2.5% interest this summer. If you want to have =C2=A3100 in 3=
0 years, and bonds pay 2.5%, you=E2=80=99ll need to put aside about =C2=A34=
8 now, which will grow at 2.5% over 30 years into =C2=A3100.
  [2]
=C2=A0If you are a company or government, you might not be jazzed about put=
ting aside almost half the money now to pay pension obligations in 30 years=
. What if you bought some stocks instead? If stocks return 8% a year on ave=
rage, you can put aside just =C2=A310 now to get back =C2=A3100 in 30 years=
. That=E2=80=99s a much better deal, for you,=C2=A0now.=C2=A0Of course the =
gilts pay 2.5% guaranteed, while the 8% stock-market return is just a guess=
; in 30 years, you (and your pension beneficiaries) might regret your riski=
er choice. But it saves you money now, and it=E2=80=99ll=C2=A0probably=C2=
=A0work out fine. Or, you know, you do some mix of super-safe gilts and ris=
kier corporate bonds and=C2=A0stocks, etc., still targeting =C2=A3100 in 30=
 years but putting less money in now and taking more risk to get there.
Second, a=C2=A0financial-stability point: Structurally, pensions=C2=A0are a=
bout the safest form of investing. Most big investors in financial markets =
are, to some degree or other, structurally short-term, in ways that make ma=
rkets fragile. Banks borrow most of their money short-term (from depositors=
, from capital markets), and if there=E2=80=99s a run on the bank then the =
bank will need to dump assets to pay back depositors.=C2=A0Mutual funds let=
 their investors take money out every day, and if a lot of investors want o=
ut then the funds will have to dump stocks to give them their money back. H=
edge funds let investors take money out and also tend to borrow money from =
prime brokers; if their assets go down then they will get margin calls from=
 brokers and will have to sell assets to meet them. The common theme is:
=09You buy some assets with other people=E2=80=99s money.=09The assets go d=
own.=09The people=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0depositors, investors, prime brokers =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0call=C2=A0you up and say=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cyou used my money to =
buy assets, and the assets went down, so now I want my money back.=E2=80=9D=
=09They have the right to do that.=09You have to sell assets to pay them ba=
ck.=09This makes the price of the assets go down more.=09Go to Step 2.

More or less every bad story in financial markets is this story, a =E2=80=
=9Cdeleveraging=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Crun on the bank.=E2=80=9D Pensions are=
=C2=A0immune=C2=A0to this. Pension funds own assets (gilts, stocks, etc.) w=
ith other people=E2=80=99s money, in the sense that they are ultimately sup=
posed to use those assets to pay benefits to pensioners. But the beneficiar=
ies can=E2=80=99t take their money out if the fund has a bad year. They jus=
t have to wait. There are no runs on pensions. The pension has to come up w=
ith =C2=A3100 in 30 years, but that=E2=80=99s it; it can=E2=80=99t be force=
d to sell early along the way.
This means, for one thing, that if you run a pension=C2=A0you=C2=A0can conf=
idently=C2=A0invest in risky assets like stocks: If stocks go down one year=
, you can make it up next year; you=E2=80=99re not going to have to shut do=
wn your pension fund because investors withdraw money after a year of bad r=
eturns. It also means that pensions are not supposed to destabilize financi=
al markets: They are long-term investors and are not forced to sell when ma=
rkets go down.
Third, an accounting point. Take the simple model of a pension: You buy a b=
ond today to pay =C2=A3100 in 30 years. I said above =E2=80=94=C2=A0with so=
me simplification =E2=80=94=C2=A0that you pay about =C2=A348 for that bond.=
 That is the value of that bond: The value of getting=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 =
years is=C2=A0=C2=A348 today. How do you account for that? What does the ba=
lance sheet look like? At some conceptual level, the balance sheet looks li=
ke =E2=80=9Cin 30 years I will have pension liabilities of=C2=A0=C2=A3100 a=
nd assets of=C2=A0=C2=A3100,=E2=80=9D so it balances. But in practice accou=
nting doesn=E2=80=99t work that way. In practice you will record the value =
of the bond as an asset, today, at=C2=A0=C2=A348. But by the same logic, yo=
u will record the value of your=C2=A0liability=C2=A0at=C2=A0=C2=A348: The c=
ost of paying=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 years is=C2=A0=C2=A348 today, so you hav=
e assets of=C2=A0=C2=A348 and liabilities of=C2=A0=C2=A348 and it all balan=
ces.
What happens if interest rates change? Let=E2=80=99s say that the interest =
rate on 30-year gilts falls to 2%. This means that the market value of your=
 bond goes up, to about=C2=A0=C2=A355. Do you have a windfall profit? Can y=
ou sell a portion of the bond? No, of course not. The market value of your =
bond has gone up, but you don=E2=80=99t care about that. The bond, for you,=
 is a long-term, hold-to-maturity investment. For you, the bond pays=C2=A0=
=C2=A3100 in 30 years; you don=E2=80=99t care about its market price now. B=
ut by the same logic, the=C2=A0present value of your liabilities=C2=A0goes =
up: Your obligation to pay=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 years is now =E2=80=9Cworth=
=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=C2=A355, using a 2% discount rate. So your balance sheet st=
ill balances.
In the simple case, none of this matters and it is sort of a confusing fict=
ion. You have to pay=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 years, you have an asset that pay=
s=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 years, you=E2=80=99re done;=C2=A0market fluctuations=
 don=E2=80=99t affect you at all. Accountants will want you to record the v=
alue of your asset and the value of your liability at their discounted pres=
ent value, and that value will fluctuate with market interest rates. As rat=
es go up, the value of your bonds will go down but the discounted cost of f=
uture pension benefits will go down; as rates go down, the bonds will go up=
 but your cost will go up too. In the simple case these things will always =
offset and won=E2=80=99t trouble you very much.
But once you move beyond the simple case this gets worse. Let=E2=80=99s say=
 you have to pay=C2=A0=C2=A3100 of benefits in 30 years, and you plan to pa=
y for that using half bonds (gilts worth=C2=A0=C2=A324 today) and half stoc=
ks (stocks worth=C2=A0=C2=A35 today). If gilts yield 2.5% and stocks return=
 8% per year for 30 years, that will give you=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 years, e=
nough to pay those benefits. But=C2=A0today, you have assets of=C2=A0=C2=A3=
29 (=C2=A324 of gilts and=C2=A0=C2=A35 of stocks), and liabilities of=C2=A0=
=C2=A348 (the present value of that=C2=A0=C2=A3100 pension obligation in 30=
 years at a 2.5% discount rate). So your pension is underfunded, by =C2=A31=
9.
  [3]
 It happens! It might be fine, if you get the returns you want. But it coul=
d make you nervous. One way to overcome this nervousness is to invest in ev=
en=C2=A0riskier=C2=A0assets with higher returns, so that next year you have=
, you know,=C2=A0=C2=A333, and are less underfunded.
The bigger problem is what happens when interest rates change. Again,=C2=A0=
say that the interest rate on 30-year gilts falls to 2%. Now you have=C2=A0=
=C2=A355 of liabilities (the present value of your pension obligations disc=
ounted at 2%). The value of your gilt holdings has gone up to =C2=A327.50 a=
s rates fell. The value of your=C2=A0stock=C2=A0holdings might not have, th=
ough; stocks don=E2=80=99t move automatically with interest rates. Still, l=
et=E2=80=99s say that your stocks=C2=A0have=C2=A0gone up, by 20%, to=C2=A0=
=C2=A36. Now you have=C2=A0=C2=A355 of liabilities and=C2=A0=C2=A333.50 of =
assets. You are underfunded by=C2=A0=C2=A321.50 instead of=C2=A0=C2=A319, w=
hich is worse.=C2=A0You have =E2=80=9Clost money,=E2=80=9D in a very accoun=
ting-fiction-y sense. Your actual pension obligations (how much you need to=
 pay in 30 years) have stayed the same, and the market value of your=C2=A0a=
ssets has gone up. But your accounting statements show that you have lost m=
oney.
Notice that what this means is that, on a reasonable set of assumptions, pe=
nsions are=C2=A0short gilts:=C2=A0They lose money (in an accounting sense) =
whenever interest rates go down (and gilt prices rise), and they make money=
 (in an accounting sense) whenever interest rates go up (and gilt prices go=
 down).
  [4]
 Notice also how counterintuitive this is: In its simplest form, a pension =
fund just=C2=A0is a pile of gilts. The basic default move for a pension man=
ager is to take a bunch of money and put it in gilts. Intuitively, she is l=
ong gilts: She has a pile of government bonds, and as rates go down the val=
ue of her holdings goes up. But as long as she doesn=E2=80=99t put all of i=
t in gilts, and as long as the pension is=C2=A0underfunded, then she is=C2=
=A0as an accounting matter=C2=A0short gilts.=C2=A0
I said above that pension funds are unusually=C2=A0insensitive=C2=A0to shor=
t-term market moves: Nobody in the pension can ask for their money back for=
 30 years, so if the pension fund has a bad year it won=E2=80=99t face with=
drawals and have to dump assets. Still, pension managers=C2=A0are sensitive=
 to accounting. If your job is to manage a pension, you want to go to your =
bosses at the end of the year and say =E2=80=9Cthis pension is now 5% less =
underfunded than it was last year.=E2=80=9D And if you have to instead say =
=E2=80=9Cthis pension is now 5%=C2=A0more=C2=A0underfunded than it was last=
 year,=E2=80=9D you are sad and maybe fired; if the pension gets too underf=
unded your regulator will step in. You want to avoid that.
And so the way you will approach your job is something like:
=09You will try to beat your benchmark, buying stocks and higher-yielding b=
onds to try to grow the value of your assets.=09You will=C2=A0hedge=C2=A0th=
e risk of rates going=C2=A0down. If rates go down, your liabilities will ri=
se (faster than your assets); you are short gilts. You want to do something=
 to minimize this risk.

The way to do that hedging is basically to get really=C2=A0long=C2=A0gilts =
in a leveraged way. If you have=C2=A0=C2=A329 of assets, you might invest t=
hem like this:
=09=C2=A324 in gilts,=09=C2=A35 in stocks, and=09borrow another=C2=A0=C2=A3=
24 and put that in gilts too.
  [5]



That way, if rates go down, the value of your portfolio goes up to match th=
e increasing value of your liabilities. So you are hedged. You were short g=
ilts, as an accounting matter, and you=E2=80=99ve solved that by borrowing =
money to buy more gilts.=C2=A0In practice, the way you have borrowed this m=
oney is probably not by actually getting a loan and buying gilts but by doi=
ng some sort of derivative (interest-rate swap, etc.) with a bank, where th=
e bank pays you if rates go down and you pay the bank if rates go up. And y=
ou have posted some collateral with the bank, and as interest rates move up=
 or down you post more or less collateral.=C2=A0
This all makes total sense, in its way. But notice that you now=C2=A0have b=
orrowed short-term money to buy volatile financial assets. The thing that w=
as so good about pension funds =E2=80=94=C2=A0their structural long-termism=
, the fact that you can=E2=80=99t have a run on a pension fund:=C2=A0You=E2=
=80=99ve ruined that! Now, if interest rates go up (gilts go down), your ba=
nk will call you up and say =E2=80=9Cyou used our money to buy assets, and =
the assets went down, so you need to give us some money back.=E2=80=9D And =
then you have to sell a bunch of your assets =E2=80=94=C2=A0the gilts and s=
tocks that you own =E2=80=94=C2=A0to pay off those margin calls. Through th=
e magic of derivatives you have transformed your safe boring long-term pens=
ion fund into a risky leveraged vehicle that could get blown up by market m=

I know this is bad but I find something aesthetically beautiful about it. I=
f you have a pot of money that is immune to bank runs, over time, modern fi=
nance will find a way to make it vulnerable to bank runs. That is=C2=A0an e=
mergent property of modern finance. No one sits down and says =E2=80=9Clet=
=E2=80=99s make pension funds vulnerable to bank runs!=E2=80=9D Finance, as=
 an abstract entity, just sort of does that on its own.

The basic rule in the US is that if you learn some secret information=C2=A0=
about a stock, and you have an=C2=A0obligation to keep it secret, and you u=

ion to someone else=C2=A0so that=C2=A0they=C2=A0can trade the stock, then y=
ou have committed insider trading and you get in trouble.

=C2=A0The classic case is that you work at a public company and find out so=
mething about the company (good or bad earnings, a merger) before it is pub=
lic and trade on that, but there are other cases. If you work at the compan=
y=E2=80=99s law firm, or if you=E2=80=99re the chief executive officer=E2=
=80=99s psychotherapist, or a regulator, you can learn things and be obliga=
ted to keep them secret. And if you=C2=A0trade on them instead you get in t=
rouble.
This is a rule of=C2=A0securities law, though sort of an unwritten one: The=
re is no specific statute banning insider trading, but there is a long trad=
ition of treating it as a form of securities fraud. Using that secret insid=
e information to trade stock is a form of=C2=A0fraud, on someone. (It=E2=80=
=99s not always clear who: The people on the other side of your trades? The=
 people whose information you misused? Both?)=C2=A0
By analogy, you might assume that using secret inside information to trade=
=C2=A0anything else=C2=A0is also a form of fraud. We   talked about this in=

f OpenSea, a=C2=A0marketplace for nonfungible tokens; the employee allegedl=
y knew in advance which NFTs would be advertised on OpenSea=E2=80=99s homep=
age and bought them so he could flip them at a profit. NFTs are (probably) =
not securities, so this is not securities fraud, so it=E2=80=99s not classi=
c insider trading. But it=E2=80=99s so much=C2=A0like=C2=A0insider trading =
that prosecutors charged him with wire fraud anyway. If insider trading sec=

Here is a weird tension in marketing crypto tokens. If you run some crypto-=
y project, and it issues a token, and the token is not actually useful for =
doing anything on your crypto-y platform,=C2=A0one thing that you can do is=
 go around saying =E2=80=9Cthis token has no cash value and will not apprec=
iate, this is for pure entertainment purposes only.=E2=80=9D Another thing =
you can do is go around saying =E2=80=9Cthis token is going to be hugely va=
luable as our crypto project takes off, buy it now and it will go up.=E2=80=
=9D
The problem with the first approach is that if you consistently and clearly=
 say =E2=80=9Cthis token is worthless,=E2=80=9D probably no one will buy it=
. (This is not always=C2=A0true =E2=80=94=C2=A0people might buy tokens for =
a meme, or for aesthetics =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it is a risk.)=C2=A0The proble=
m with the second approach is that if you say =E2=80=9Cthis token represent=
s a bet on the success of our business venture,=E2=80=9D then it is   proba=
bly a security under=C2=A0traditional US securities law, and the US Securit=
ies and Exchange Commission will sue you for securities fraud unless (1) yo=
u have registered the security or exempted it from registration (you haven=
=E2=80=99t) and (2) you are telling the truth about your business venture (=
are=C2=A0you?). The first approach won=E2=80=99t make you any money; the se=
cond approach will get you in trouble.
There are various artful ways to split the difference. One rough one is:
=09You consistently say, in boilerplate, =E2=80=9Cthis token has no value a=
nd is not an investment.=E2=80=9D=09You go around doing podcast interviews =
where you are like =E2=80=9Cwhat a great business we have, we keep making m=
ore money, oh there is also a token, how about that.=E2=80=9D You don=E2=80=
=99t=C2=A0say=C2=A0that the token represents a bet on the business, but you=
 vaguely imply it.=09You do a lot of wash trading, where you buy the token =
from yourself and then sell it back to yourself, at ever-increasing prices,=
 creating the impression that its value is going up.=C2=A0

And then you have not quite said that it=E2=80=99s an investment, so it=E2=
=80=99s arguably not a security, but maybe you have gotten enough people in=
terested in it that you can sell it to them and get some money.

n idea. Here=E2=80=99s an SEC  enforcement action from yesterday:

rading Commission? prosecutors?) might go after you for=C2=A0fraud, but the=
=C2=A0SEC=C2=A0won=E2=80=99t go after you for=C2=A0securities fraud=C2=A0be=
cause the fraud that you are doing is not to a security. If your model is t=
hat the SEC has way less of a sense of humor about these things than anyone=
 else, then that might be a good outcome.

 =20
   =20
      Do Kwon
   =20
 =20

In entirely related news, let=E2=80=99s check in with Do Kwon, the founder =
of the blown-up Terra blockchain and its token Luna,  in an undisclosed loc=
ation:

Mr. Kwon faces charges of violating South Korea=E2=80=99s capital market la=
w, according to the country=E2=80=99s Yonhap News Agency. But Terraform Lab=
s argued that the law would only apply to Luna if it were a security. If it=
 isn=E2=80=99t a security, then Mr. Kwon and his firm didn=E2=80=99t do any=
thing illegal, the Terraform Labs spokesman said.
Terraform Labs=E2=80=99 argument that Luna isn=E2=80=99t a security stems f=
rom the murky regulatory status of cryptocurrencies, which many countries a=
round the world have struggled with, including the United States. Officials=
, such as Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen, have cited the collapse of Terra=
USD as evidence for the need for stronger crypto regulation.
The Terraform Labs spokesman suggested that South Korean prosecutors had ex=
panded the definition of a security in response to public pressure over the=
 collapse of Luna, which has since been renamed =E2=80=9CLuna Classic=E2=80=
=9D as Mr. Kwon has sought to launch a new version of the cryptocurrency.
=E2=80=9CWe believe, as do most in industry, that Luna Classic is not, and =
has never been, a security, despite any changes in interpretation that Kore=
an financial officials may have recently adopted,=E2=80=9D Terraform Labs=
=E2=80=99 spokesman said.

If you thought that Luna was an investment in the collaborative effort to b=
uild the value of the Terra blockchain, then (1) it=E2=80=99s a security
  [7]
 and (2) maybe you wanted to buy it, which is why Luna had a market capital=
ization in the tens of billions of dollars. If you thought that Luna was ju=
st, like, an electronic token with no investment thesis, then maybe it wasn=
=E2=80=99t a security =E2=80=94=C2=A0but why were you buying it?

  [1] In real life you need to pay out a bunch of money at many different p=
oints in the future, and those government bonds pay interest between now an=
d 30 years from now, and you have some actuarial risk about how long your b=
eneficiaries will live, etc. You=E2=80=99ll buy lots of bonds maturing at l=
ots of points, and reinvest interest, and have some uncertainty about your =
liabilities. And I am entirely ignoring the effects of inflation, inflation=
-indexing of benefits, inflation-linked bonds, etc. But simplifying it as =
=E2=80=9Cyou have to pay a bunch of money in 30 years, so buy assets that w=
ill provide that amount of money in 30 years=E2=80=9D is fine.


  [2] This is just the price of a zero-coupon 30-year bond at a 2.5% yield =
paying =C2=A3100 at maturity, i.e. =3DPV(.025,30,0,-100) in Excel. There ar=
e some embedded assumptions there and don=E2=80=99t take it too seriously, =
but in fact a UK gilt principal strip maturing in 2049 (UKTR 0 12/07/49 Gov=
t on Bloomberg) was trading in the sort of 45-51 area this June and July so=
 good enough.


  [3] I=E2=80=99m assuming for simplicity that you discount your liabilitie=
s at the risk-free rate, which is not really right, but directionally this =
is all pretty much true even if you use some spread to the risk-free rate.


  [4] The basic driver of this in my simplified math is the underfunding. I=
f you have a pension with =C2=A329 of assets and =C2=A348 of liabilities, a=
nd discount rates go down, then the values of your assets and liabilities b=
oth increase; even if they increase by the same proportion (even if the ass=
ets increase by *more*, proportionally), the liabilities are bigger so your=
 underfunding gets larger.=20


  [5] No science to this number, and you=E2=80=99d probably do a bit less i=
f your stocks are correlated with rates.


  [6] There are tons of nuances to this rule =E2=80=94 I say =E2=80=9Cstock=
=E2=80=9D but also mean bonds and options; if you give the information to s=
omeone else, instead of selling it, you   might or might not also be in tro=
uble =E2=80=94 and none of this is legal advice, but this is the basic rule=
.


  [7] I=E2=80=99m being loose here =E2=80=94 I have been talking about US l=
aw, and this is an issue of South Korean law. Nonetheless the basic idea of=
 =E2=80=9Cis this an investment in a business (a security) or a collectible=
 curiosity (not a security)?=E2=80=9D seems conceptually relevant no matter=
 the details of the legal regime.

<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">LDI</h2>
</td>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is a simple model of a pension fund. You =
know you will need to pay out a bunch of money=C2=A030 years from now, so y=
ou buy some 30-year government bonds and hold them to maturity. When the bo=
nds mature in 30 years, you have money, which you give to the pensioners, a=
nd you=E2=80=99re done. This model is obviously oversimplified,<a class=3D"=
footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorati=
on: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000=
; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !impo=
rtant; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a> but it=E2=80=99s a good start.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Let me make three points about this model. Fir=
st, a financial point: Doing a pension fund this way is expensive. Thirty-y=
ear UK gilts (government bonds) paid about 2.5% interest this summer. If yo=
u want to have =C2=A3100 in 30 years, and bonds pay 2.5%, you=E2=80=99ll ne=
ed to put aside about =C2=A348 now, which will grow at 2.5% over 30 years i=
nto =C2=A3100.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"col=
or: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bo=
rder-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; backgroun=
d-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>=C2=A0If you are a company or government, you might not be jazzed about=
 putting aside almost half the money now to pay pension obligations in 30 y=
ears. What if you bought some stocks instead? If stocks return 8% a year on=
 average, you can put aside just =C2=A310 now to get back =C2=A3100 in 30 y=
ears. That=E2=80=99s a much better deal, for you,=C2=A0<em>now.=C2=A0</em>O=
f course the gilts pay 2.5% guaranteed, while the 8% stock-market return is=
 just a guess; in 30 years, you (and your pension beneficiaries) might regr=
et your riskier choice. But it saves you money now, and it=E2=80=99ll=C2=A0=
<em>probably=C2=A0</em>work out fine. Or, you know, you do some mix of supe=
r-safe gilts and riskier corporate bonds and=C2=A0stocks, etc., still targe=
ting =C2=A3100 in 30 years but putting less money in now and taking more ri=
sk to get there.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Second, a=C2=A0financial-stability point: Stru=
cturally, pensions=C2=A0are about the safest form of investing. Most big in=
vestors in financial markets are, to some degree or other, structurally sho=
rt-term, in ways that make markets fragile. Banks borrow most of their mone=
y short-term (from depositors, from capital markets), and if there=E2=80=99=
s a run on the bank then the bank will need to dump assets to pay back depo=
sitors.=C2=A0Mutual funds let their investors take money out every day, and=
 if a lot of investors want out then the funds will have to dump stocks to =
give them their money back. Hedge funds let investors take money out and al=
so tend to borrow money from prime brokers; if their assets go down then th=
ey will get margin calls from brokers and will have to sell assets to meet =
them. The common theme is:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You buy some assets with other people=
=E2=80=99s money.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The assets go do=
wn.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The people=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=
=A0depositors, investors, prime brokers =E2=80=94=C2=A0call=C2=A0you up and=
 say=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cyou used my money to buy assets, and the assets went dow=
n, so now I want my money back.=E2=80=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom:=
 5px;">They have the right to do that.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5=
px;">You have to sell assets to pay them back.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-b=
ottom: 5px;">This makes the price of the assets go down more.</li>=09<li st=
yle=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Go to Step 2.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">More or less every bad story in financial mark=
ets is this story, a =E2=80=9Cdeleveraging=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Crun on the =
bank.=E2=80=9D Pensions are=C2=A0<em>immune=C2=A0</em>to this. Pension fund=
s own assets (gilts, stocks, etc.) with other people=E2=80=99s money, in th=
e sense that they are ultimately supposed to use those assets to pay benefi=
ts to pensioners. But the beneficiaries can=E2=80=99t take their money out =
if the fund has a bad year. They just have to wait. There are no runs on pe=
nsions. The pension has to come up with =C2=A3100 in 30 years, but that=E2=
=80=99s it; it can=E2=80=99t be forced to sell early along the way.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This means, for one thing, that if you run a p=
ension=C2=A0you=C2=A0<em>can </em>confidently=C2=A0invest in risky assets l=
ike stocks: If stocks go down one year, you can make it up next year; you=
=E2=80=99re not going to have to shut down your pension fund because invest=
ors withdraw money after a year of bad returns. It also means that pensions=
 are not supposed to destabilize financial markets: They are long-term inve=
stors and are not forced to sell when markets go down.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Third, an accounting point. Take the simple mo=
del of a pension: You buy a bond today to pay =C2=A3100 in 30 years. I said=
 above =E2=80=94=C2=A0with some simplification =E2=80=94=C2=A0that you pay =
about =C2=A348 for that bond. That is the value of that bond: The value of =
getting=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 years is=C2=A0=C2=A348 today. How do you accou=
nt for that? What does the balance sheet look like? At some conceptual leve=
l, the balance sheet looks like =E2=80=9Cin 30 years I will have pension li=
abilities of=C2=A0=C2=A3100 and assets of=C2=A0=C2=A3100,=E2=80=9D so it ba=
lances. But in practice accounting doesn=E2=80=99t work that way. In practi=
ce you will record the value of the bond as an asset, today, at=C2=A0=C2=A3=
48. But by the same logic, you will record the value of your=C2=A0<em>liabi=
lity=C2=A0</em>at=C2=A0=C2=A348: The cost of paying=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 ye=
ars is=C2=A0=C2=A348 today, so you have assets of=C2=A0=C2=A348 and liabili=
ties of=C2=A0=C2=A348 and it all balances.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What happens if interest rates change? Let=E2=
=80=99s say that the interest rate on 30-year gilts falls to 2%. This means=
 that the market value of your bond goes up, to about=C2=A0=C2=A355. Do you=
 have a windfall profit? Can you sell a portion of the bond? No, of course =
not. The market value of your bond has gone up, but you don=E2=80=99t care =
about that. The bond, for you, is a long-term, hold-to-maturity investment.=
 For you, the bond pays=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 years; you don=E2=80=99t care =
about its market price now. But by the same logic, the=C2=A0<em>present val=
ue of your liabilities=C2=A0</em>goes up: Your obligation to pay=C2=A0=C2=
=A3100 in 30 years is now =E2=80=9Cworth=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=C2=A355, using a 2%=
 discount rate. So your balance sheet still balances.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In the simple case, none of this matters and i=
t is sort of a confusing fiction. You have to pay=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 year=
s, you have an asset that pays=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 years, you=E2=80=99re d=
one;=C2=A0market fluctuations don=E2=80=99t affect you at all. Accountants =
will want you to record the value of your asset and the value of your liabi=
lity at their discounted present value, and that value will fluctuate with =
market interest rates. As rates go up, the value of your bonds will go down=
 but the discounted cost of future pension benefits will go down; as rates =
go down, the bonds will go up but your cost will go up too. In the simple c=
ase these things will always offset and won=E2=80=99t trouble you very much=
.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But once you move beyond the simple case this =
gets worse. Let=E2=80=99s say you have to pay=C2=A0=C2=A3100 of benefits in=
 30 years, and you plan to pay for that using half bonds (gilts worth=C2=A0=
=C2=A324 today) and half stocks (stocks worth=C2=A0=C2=A35 today). If gilts=
 yield 2.5% and stocks return 8% per year for 30 years, that will give you=
=C2=A0=C2=A3100 in 30 years, enough to pay those benefits. But=C2=A0<em>tod=
ay</em>, you have assets of=C2=A0=C2=A329 (=C2=A324 of gilts and=C2=A0=C2=
=A35 of stocks), and liabilities of=C2=A0=C2=A348 (the present value of tha=
t=C2=A0=C2=A3100 pension obligation in 30 years at a 2.5% discount rate). S=
o your pension is underfunded, by =C2=A319.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=
=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inli=
ne-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a> It happens! It might be fine, if you get the returns you want. But it =
could make you nervous. One way to overcome this nervousness is to invest i=
n even=C2=A0<em>riskier=C2=A0</em>assets with higher returns, so that next =
year you have, you know,=C2=A0=C2=A333, and are less underfunded.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The bigger problem is what happens when intere=
st rates change. Again,=C2=A0say that the interest rate on 30-year gilts fa=
lls to 2%. Now you have=C2=A0=C2=A355 of liabilities (the present value of =
your pension obligations discounted at 2%). The value of your gilt holdings=
 has gone up to =C2=A327.50 as rates fell. The value of your=C2=A0<em>stock=
=C2=A0</em>holdings might not have, though; stocks don=E2=80=99t move autom=
atically with interest rates. Still, let=E2=80=99s say that your stocks=C2=
=A0<em>have=C2=A0</em>gone up, by 20%, to=C2=A0=C2=A36. Now you have=C2=A0=
=C2=A355 of liabilities and=C2=A0=C2=A333.50 of assets. You are underfunded=
 by=C2=A0=C2=A321.50 instead of=C2=A0=C2=A319, which is worse.=C2=A0You hav=
e =E2=80=9Clost money,=E2=80=9D in a very accounting-fiction-y sense. Your =
actual pension obligations (how much you need to pay in 30 years) have stay=
ed the same, and the market value of your=C2=A0assets has gone up. But your=
 accounting statements show that you have lost money.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Notice that what this means is that, on a reas=
onable set of assumptions, pensions are=C2=A0<em>short gilts:=C2=A0</em>The=
y lose money (in an accounting sense) whenever interest rates go down (and =
gilt prices rise), and they make money (in an accounting sense) whenever in=
terest rates go up (and gilt prices go down).<a class=3D"footnote-index" hr=
ef=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importan=
t; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-st=
yle: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: in=
line-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a> Notice also how counterintuitive this is: In its simplest form, a pens=
ion fund just=C2=A0<em>is a pile of gilts</em>. The basic default move for =
a pension manager is to take a bunch of money and put it in gilts. Intuitiv=
ely, she is long gilts: She has a pile of government bonds, and as rates go=
 down the value of her holdings goes up. But as long as she doesn=E2=80=99t=
 put <em>all</em> of it in gilts, and as long as the pension is=C2=A0underf=
unded, then she is=C2=A0<em>as an accounting matter=C2=A0</em>short gilts.=
=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I said above that pension funds are unusually=
=C2=A0<em>insensitive=C2=A0</em>to short-term market moves: Nobody in the p=
ension can ask for their money back for 30 years, so if the pension fund ha=
s a bad year it won=E2=80=99t face withdrawals and have to dump assets. Sti=
ll, pension managers=C2=A0<em>are </em>sensitive to accounting. If your job=
 is to manage a pension, you want to go to your bosses at the end of the ye=
ar and say =E2=80=9Cthis pension is now 5% less underfunded than it was las=
t year.=E2=80=9D And if you have to instead say =E2=80=9Cthis pension is no=
w 5%=C2=A0<em>more=C2=A0</em>underfunded than it was last year,=E2=80=9D yo=
u are sad and maybe fired; if the pension gets too underfunded your regulat=
or will step in. You want to avoid that.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so the way you will approach your job is s=
omething like:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You will try to beat your benchmark, =
buying stocks and higher-yielding bonds to try to grow the value of your as=
sets.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You will=C2=A0<em>hedge=C2=
=A0</em>the risk of rates going=C2=A0<em>down</em>. If rates go down, your =
liabilities will rise (faster than your assets); you are short gilts. You w=
ant to do something to minimize this risk.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The way to do that hedging is basically to get=
 really=C2=A0<em>long=C2=A0</em>gilts in a leveraged way. If you have=C2=A0=
=C2=A329 of assets, you might invest them like this:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=C2=A324 in gilts,</li>=09<li style=
=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=C2=A35 in stocks, and</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-=
bottom: 5px;">borrow another=C2=A0=C2=A324 and put that in gilts too.<a cla=
ss=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-de=
coration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #=
000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent=
 !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a>
</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That way, if rates go down, the value of your =
portfolio goes up to match the increasing value of your liabilities. So you=
 are hedged. You were short gilts, as an accounting matter, and you=E2=80=
=99ve solved that by borrowing money to buy more gilts.=C2=A0In practice, t=
he way you have borrowed this money is probably not by actually getting a l=
oan and buying gilts but by doing some sort of derivative (interest-rate sw=
ap, etc.) with a bank, where the bank pays you if rates go down and you pay=
 the bank if rates go up. And you have posted some collateral with the bank=
, and as interest rates move up or down you post more or less collateral.=
=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This all makes total sense, in its way. But no=
tice that you now=C2=A0<em>have borrowed short-term money to buy volatile f=
inancial assets</em>. The thing that was so good about pension funds =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0their structural long-termism, the fact that you can=E2=80=99t hav=
e a run on a pension fund:=C2=A0You=E2=80=99ve ruined that! Now, if interes=
t rates go up (gilts go down), your bank will call you up and say =E2=80=9C=
you used our money to buy assets, and the assets went down, so you need to =
give us some money back.=E2=80=9D And then you have to sell a bunch of your=
 assets =E2=80=94=C2=A0the gilts and stocks that you own =E2=80=94=C2=A0to =
pay off those margin calls. Through the magic of derivatives you have trans=
formed your safe boring long-term pension fund into a risky leveraged vehic=
le that could get blown up by market moves.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I know this is bad but I find something aesthe=
tically beautiful about it. If you have a pot of money that is immune to ba=
nk runs, over time, modern finance will find a way to make it vulnerable to=
 bank runs. That is=C2=A0an emergent property of modern finance. No one sit=
s down and says =E2=80=9Clet=E2=80=99s make pension funds vulnerable to ban=
k runs!=E2=80=9D Finance, as an abstract entity, just sort of does that on =
its own.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway, as I said above, 30-year UK gilt rates=
 were about 2.5% this summer. They got to nearly 5% this week, and were at =
about 3.9% at 9 a.m. New York time today. You can fill in the rest. Here=C2=
=A0are=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/292122=
80.288530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDktM=
jgvdGhlLXVrLXBlbnNpb24tcHJvYmxlbS10aGF0LXRocmVhdGVuZWQtdG8td3JlY2stdGhlLWdp=
bHQtbWFya2V0P2NtcGlkPUJCRDA5MjkyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXR=
tX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDkyOSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdH=
VmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8c29f3cd" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D=
"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Loukia Gyftopoulou and Greg=
 Ritchie at Bloomberg News</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Fund managers running billions for pension fun=
ds faced collateral calls on strategies meant to give them exposure to long=
-dated assets to help match obligations that can extend decades. The so-cal=
led liability-driven investment, or LDI, funds were forced to post more col=
lateral after receiving margin calls when gilt prices collapsed.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The central bank stepped in Wednesday after th=
e calls threatened to push the gilt market into a downward spiral. The BOE =
had been warned by investment banks and fund managers in recent days that t=
he collateral requirements could trigger a gilt crash, according to a perso=
n familiar with the BOE=E2=80=99s deliberations before they stepped in.=C2=
=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CThe BOE intervention was required to =
prevent a vicious cycle becoming even more dangerous for pension funds forc=
ed to sell their gilt exposures,=E2=80=9D Calum Mackenzie, an investment pa=
rtner at Aon, said after the BOE intervention. =E2=80=9CThe market=E2=80=99=
s swift and significant reaction underlined the big risk faced by pension f=
unds who have had or who could have had their liability hedges reduced.=E2=
=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Firms including BlackRock Inc., Legal & Ge=
neral Group Plc and Schroders Plc manage LDI funds on behalf of pension cli=
ents. The pension firms use them to match their liabilities with their asse=
ts, often using derivatives.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The size of the LDI market has exploded over t=
he past decade. The amount of liabilities held by UK pension funds that hav=
e been hedged with LDI strategies has tripled in size to =C2=A31.5 trillion=
 in the 10 years through 2020, according to the Investment Association. The=
se trades are typically used by defined benefit pension schemes. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When yields fall the funds receive margin and =
when yields rise they typically have to post more collateral. After the spi=
ke in gilt yields on Friday and into this week, LDI fund managers were hit =
by margin calls from their investment banks.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">LDI collateral buffers are partly set using hi=
storical data to build models based on the likely probability of gilt price=
 movements, according to Shalin Bhagwan, head of pension advisory at DWS Gr=
oup. The sudden recent surge in gilt yields =E2=80=9Cblew through the model=
s and the collateral buffers,=E2=80=9D he said.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And here is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=
bergbusiness.com/click/29212280.288530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQv=
NzU2ZTgxZDEtYjJhNi00NTgwLTkwNTQtMjA2Mzg2MzUzYzRl/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB6=
bbc96fe" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgrou=
nd-color: #ccc;"> the Financial Times</a>=C2=A0on the BOE=E2=80=99s interve=
ntion:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The bank stressed that it was not seeking to l=
ower long-term government borrowing costs. Instead it wanted to buy time to=
 prevent a vicious circle in which pension funds have to sell gilts immedia=
tely to meet demands for cash from their creditors. That process had put pe=
nsion funds at risk of insolvency, because the mass sell-offs pushed down f=
urther the price of gilts held by funds as assets, requiring them to stump =
up even more cash. =E2=80=9CAt some point this morning I was worried this w=
as the beginning of the end,=E2=80=9D said a senior London-based banker, ad=
ding that at one point on Wednesday morning there were no buyers of long-da=
ted UK gilts. =E2=80=9CIt was not quite a Lehman moment. But it got close.=
=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CIf there was no intervention today, g=
ilt yields could have gone up to 7-8 per cent from 4.5 per cent this mornin=
g and in that situation around 90 per cent of UK pension funds would have r=
un out of collateral,=E2=80=9D said Kerrin Rosenberg, Cardano Investment ch=
ief executive. =E2=80=9CThey would have been wiped out.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And=C2=A0FT Alphaville has two very good expla=
iners of the LDI problem, one by <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusi=
ness.com/click/29212280.288530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQvMDM4YjMw=
YzMtZjU1MC00Y2MwLTkzZWQtOTE1NDAyMWQ2ZWUy/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf297ffd0"=
 itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"=
color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px;=
 border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color=
: #ccc;"> Toby Nangle</a> and another by <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=
bergbusiness.com/click/29212280.288530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQv=
ZjRhNzI4YTUtMDE3OS00OGJkLWIyOTItZjQ4ZTMwZjg2MDNj/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd=
0c6b1a5" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgrou=
nd-color: #ccc;"> Alex Scaggs and Louis Ashworth</a>, which I have drawn on=
 here. And here is Nangle=E2=80=99s=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=
bergbusiness.com/click/29212280.288530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQv=
ODM5Mjc2ODgtZTBkMS00OTM0LThkOTEtZTI3OWRhNmQ2YjZj/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8=
951de6c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgrou=
nd-color: #ccc;">prescient LDI explainer</a> from July. Modern finance made=
 UK pensions vulnerable to runs, and then there was a run on those pensions=
, and the Bank of England had to step in to buy gilts to save them, because=
 that=E2=80=99s what happens in a bank run.</p>

5px;">Bourbon insider trading</h2>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The basic rule in the US is that if you learn =
some secret information=C2=A0<em>about a stock</em>, and you have an=C2=A0<=
em>obligation to keep it secret</em>, and you use that information to=C2=A0=
<em>trade the stock</em>, or you=C2=A0<em>sell that information to someone =
else=C2=A0</em>so that=C2=A0<em>they=C2=A0</em>can trade the stock, then yo=
u have committed insider trading and you get in trouble.<a class=3D"footnot=
e-index" href=3D"#footnote-6" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; =
display: inline-block;">
<span>[6]</span>
</a>=C2=A0The classic case is that you work at a public company and find ou=
t something about the company (good or bad earnings, a merger) before it is=
 public and trade on that, but there are other cases. If you work at the co=
mpany=E2=80=99s law firm, or if you=E2=80=99re the chief executive officer=
=E2=80=99s psychotherapist, or a regulator, you can learn things and be obl=
igated to keep them secret. And if you=C2=A0trade on them instead you get i=
n trouble.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is a rule of=C2=A0<em>securities law</em>=
, though sort of an unwritten one: There is no specific statute banning ins=
ider trading, but there is a long tradition of treating it as a form of sec=
urities fraud. Using that secret inside information to trade stock is a for=
m of=C2=A0<em>fraud</em>, on someone. (It=E2=80=99s not always clear who: T=
he people on the other side of your trades? The people whose information yo=
u misused? Both?)=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">By analogy, you might assume that using secret=
 inside information to trade=C2=A0<em>anything else=C2=A0</em>is also a for=
m of fraud. We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/292=
12280.288530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMj=
ItMDYtMDIvZG9uLXQtaW5zaWRlci10cmFkZS1uZnRzP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA5MjkyMl9NT05FWVNUV=
UZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDky=
OSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8c88ca4c" itemp=
rop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color:=
 #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; borde=
r-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc=
;">  talked about this in June</a> in the context of an insider trading cas=
e against a former employee of OpenSea, a=C2=A0marketplace for nonfungible =
tokens; the employee allegedly knew in advance which NFTs would be advertis=
ed on OpenSea=E2=80=99s homepage and bought them so he could flip them at a=
 profit. NFTs are (probably) not securities, so this is not securities frau=
d, so it=E2=80=99s not classic insider trading. But it=E2=80=99s so much=C2=
=A0<em>like=C2=A0</em>insider trading that prosecutors charged him with wir=
e fraud anyway. If insider trading securities is securities fraud, then ins=
ider trading non-securities is wire fraud.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway it=E2=80=99s not wire fraud but here=E2=
=80=99s a <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29212280=
.288530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud2FzaGluZ3RvbnBvc3QuY29tL2RjLW1kLXZhLzIwMjIvMDkvMjA=
vYm91cmJvbi1ibGFjay1tYXJrZXQtY3JpbWUvJUMyJUEw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8ecc=
2289" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" styl=
e=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;"> bourbon insider trading prosecution</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In an unusual criminal case unfolding outside =
Richmond, a former employee of the state=E2=80=99s Alcoholic Beverage Contr=
ol Authority (ABC)<strong>=C2=A0</strong>has admitted to working with [Rob]=
 Adams<strong>=C2=A0</strong>to sell distribution information<strong>=C2=A0=
</strong>that would allow bourbon fans to scoop up the limited supply<stron=
g>=C2=A0</strong>of choice bottles.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Adams, 45, has been charged with embezzlement =
and other felonies. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0The investigation concluded Adams learne=
d that Garcia was an employee at the ABC, Stock said. As a retail specialis=
t making $16.53 an hour, Stock said, Garcia had access to the internal list=
 guiding the distribution of Angel=E2=80=99s Envy Cask Strength, Old Fitzge=
rald 17-year Bottled in Bond, WhistlePig 18-year Double Malt Rye and other =
sought-after bottles.</p>
</blockquote>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CThey said, =E2=80=98Why don=E2=80=99t=
 we make a little money on the side,=E2=80=99 =E2=80=9D Stock alleged in an=
 interview.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Stock said during Monday=E2=80=99s hearing tha=
t Adams paid Garcia $600 for access to the list and promised him bottles of=
 alcohol. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Vaughan Jones, an attorney for Adams, said his=
 client did distribute the list, but did not commit a crime because he had =
no legal obligation to keep the information secret.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CMy client was never an employee of AB=
C,=E2=80=9D Jones said. =E2=80=9CHe never accessed the information. He was =
just giving it out.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yeah I mean in securities law this stuff is al=
l reasonably settled, and lots of people go to jail for insider trading wit=
hout themselves being insiders; paying an insider $600 for inside informati=
on more than qualifies. But=C2=A0in state-law bourbon embezzlement cases it=
 is perhaps less clear.</p>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is a weird tension in marketing crypto to=
kens. If you run some crypto-y project, and it issues a token, and the toke=
n is not actually useful for doing anything on your crypto-y platform,=C2=
=A0one thing that you can do is go around saying =E2=80=9Cthis token has no=
 cash value and will not appreciate, this is for pure entertainment purpose=
s only.=E2=80=9D Another thing you can do is go around saying =E2=80=9Cthis=
 token is going to be hugely valuable as our crypto project takes off, buy =
it now and it will go up.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The problem with the first approach is that if=
 you consistently and clearly say =E2=80=9Cthis token is worthless,=E2=80=
=9D probably no one will buy it. (This is not <em>always=C2=A0</em>true =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0people might buy tokens for a meme, or for aesthetics =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0but it is a risk.)=C2=A0The problem with the second approach is that =
if you say =E2=80=9Cthis token represents a bet on the success of our busin=
ess venture,=E2=80=9D then it is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusi=
ness.com/click/29212280.288530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9u=
L2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDktMDgvZ2FyeS1nZW5zbGVyLXdhbnRzLXRvLXJlZ3VsYXRlLWNyeXB=
0bz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwOTI5MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2=
U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjA5MjkmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f0=
7ceef4b8524a319a23cB47bf68fc" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope=
" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importa=
nt; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-s=
tyle: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  probably a <em>security</em> under=
=C2=A0traditional US securities law</a>, and the US Securities and Exchange=
 Commission will sue you for securities fraud unless (1) you have registere=
d the security or exempted it from registration (you haven=E2=80=99t) and (=
2) you are telling the truth about your business venture (<em>are=C2=A0</em=
>you?). The first approach won=E2=80=99t make you any money; the second app=
roach will get you in trouble.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are various artful ways to split the dif=
ference. One rough one is:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You consistently say, in boilerplate,=
 =E2=80=9Cthis token has no value and is not an investment.=E2=80=9D</li>=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You go around doing podcast interviews=
 where you are like =E2=80=9Cwhat a great business we have, we keep making =
more money, oh there is also a token, how about that.=E2=80=9D You don=E2=
=80=99t=C2=A0<em>say=C2=A0</em>that the token represents a bet on the busin=
ess, but you vaguely imply it.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You=
 do a lot of wash trading, where you buy the token from yourself and then s=
ell it back to yourself, at ever-increasing prices, creating the impression=
 that its value is going up.=C2=A0</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And then you have not quite said that it=E2=80=
=99s an investment, so it=E2=80=99s arguably not a security, but maybe you =
have gotten enough people interested in it that you can sell it to them and=
 get some money.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I am not saying that that will work, or that i=
t is a good idea, but it is <em>an</em> idea. Here=E2=80=99s an SEC <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29212280.288530/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMi0xNzU/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB1be76b93" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bl=
ank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;"> enforcement action from yesterday</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Securities and Exchange Commission today a=
nnounced charges against The Hydrogen Technology Corporation, its former CE=
O, Michael Ross Kane, and Tyler Ostern, the CEO of Moonwalkers Trading Limi=
ted, a self-described =E2=80=9Cmarket making=E2=80=9D firm, for their roles=
 in effectuating the unregistered offers and sales of crypto asset securiti=
es called =E2=80=9CHydro=E2=80=9D and for perpetrating a scheme to manipula=
te the trading volume and price of those securities, which yielded more tha=
n $2 million for Hydrogen.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The SEC=E2=80=99s complaint alleges that start=
ing in January 2018, Kane and Hydrogen, a New York-based financial technolo=
gy company, created its Hydro token and then publicly distributed the token=
 through various methods: =C2=A0an =E2=80=9Cairdrop,=E2=80=9D which is esse=
ntially giving away Hydro to the public; bounty programs, which paid the to=
ken to individuals in exchange for promoting it; employee compensation; and=
 direct sales on crypto asset trading platforms.=C2=A0 The complaint furthe=
r alleges that, after distributing the token in those ways, Kane and Hydrog=
en hired Moonwalkers, a South Africa-based firm, in October 2018, to create=
 the false appearance of robust market activity for Hydro through the use o=
f its customized trading software or =E2=80=9Cbot=E2=80=9D and then selling=
 Hydro into that artificially inflated market for profit on Hydrogen=E2=80=
=99s behalf.=C2=A0 Hydrogen allegedly reaped profits of more than $2 millio=
n as a result of the defendants=E2=80=99 conduct.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">They gave the tokens away for free (including =
to themselves), and were careful-ish about not advertising them as an inves=
tment. From <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/292122=
80.288530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9saXRpZ2F0aW9uL2NvbXBsYWludHMvMjAyMi9jb=
21wLXByMjAyMi0xNzUucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB299ad19a" itemprop=3D"Stor=
yLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; t=
ext-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-co=
lor: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> the SEC=
 complaint</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Hydrogen and Kane publicly marketed Hydro as a=
 so-called =E2=80=9Cutility=E2=80=9D token on the company=E2=80=99s website=
 and its social media pages and channels, initially claiming that it would=
=C2=A0function as an =E2=80=9CAPI key=E2=80=9D within Hydrogen=E2=80=99s ex=
isting non-blockchain API business. However, at no point during the Relevan=
t Period, including during the offers and sales of Hydro, could the token b=
e used within Hydrogen=E2=80=99s existing non-blockchain API. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">While Hydrogen and Kane worked to have Hydro l=
isted on crypto asset trading platforms, the company fielded numerous quest=
ions on its social media pages and channels from Hydro recipients and purch=
asers about the token=E2=80=99s value and whether and when Hydrogen expecte=
d the token=E2=80=99s price to increase.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">At Kane=E2=80=99s direction, Hydrogen created =
a set of scripted responses to these questions, being careful not to expres=
sly state that the Hydro token would increase in value.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But instead they allegedly created that impres=
sion through trading:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Kane began selling the company=E2=80=99s Hydro=
 through crypto asset trading platforms in May 2018, but soon after learned=
 that selling a significant volume of Hydro would depress the token=E2=80=
=99s price and hinder his efforts to raise much-needed capital for Hydrogen=
. As a result, in October 2018, Kane privately hired and directed Ostern an=
d his company, Moonwalkers, a self-described crypto asset =E2=80=9Cmarket m=
aker,=E2=80=9D to manipulate Hydro=E2=80=99s trading price and volume so th=
at the company=E2=80=99s Hydro sales would be more profitable. Moonwalkers =
did so by creating the false appearance of robust Hydro trading and artific=
ially propping up the token=E2=80=99s price.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Specifically, at Kane=E2=80=99s direction, Ost=
ern used a customized trading bot (a computer program that automates trades=
) to sell the company=E2=80=99s Hydro through Kane=E2=80=99s personal tradi=
ng accounts on crypto asset trading platforms. Among other manipulation tac=
tics, Ostern placed and canceled both buy and sell orders at random increme=
nts to artificially inflate the Hydro token=E2=80=99s trade volume and pric=
e, thereby enabling sales of the company=E2=80=99s Hydro to be more profita=
ble.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Ostern provided Kane and Hydrogen with regular=
 updates on his market manipulation efforts. For example, on October 11, 20=
18, just days after the Hydro market manipulation began, Ostern told Kane t=
hat he was =E2=80=9Cstarting off slow, trying to keep the sell pressure min=
imal until [he could] build enough capital to really get the market moving =
upward=E2=80=9D and indicated that they would =E2=80=9Chave plenty of excus=
es to pump price and sell into the FOMO [fear of missing out] guys down the=
 road.=E2=80=9D Two weeks later, Ostern told Kane about his =E2=80=9Cvolume=
 shenanigans=E2=80=9D on a popular, high-volume crypto asset trading platfo=
rm, and bragged that it had taken his bot =E2=80=9Cabout 3 seconds=E2=80=9D=
 to generate the illusion that =E2=80=9Ca million=E2=80=9D Hydro tokens had=
 been bought and sold=E2=80=94=E2=80=9C[a]round half=E2=80=9D of which Oste=
rn admitted was =E2=80=9Cfake.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CThe name =E2=80=98Moonwalkers,=E2=80=
=99=E2=80=9D explains the SEC, =E2=80=9Cderives from the expression =E2=80=
=98going to the moon,=E2=80=99 which is used by crypto enthusiasts and issu=
ers to describe the potential significant appreciation in price or value of=
 crypto assets,=E2=80=9D terrific. You=C2=A0<em>walk </em>a token to the=C2=
=A0<em>moon=C2=A0</em>with fake trading I guess.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This didn=E2=80=99t really work, I don=E2=80=
=99t think, in that the SEC brought the case, but I want to highlight the t=
hought process. If (1) the token is absolutely valueless and has nothing to=
 do with any operating business, and (2) you=C2=A0<em>say=C2=A0</em>that cl=
early and consistently, then you have a decent argument that it is not a =
=E2=80=9Csecurity.=E2=80=9D The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusin=
ess.com/click/29212280.288530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL25ld=
3NsZXR0ZXJzLzIwMjEtMDgtMDkvbW9uZXktc3R1ZmYtdGhlLXNlYy1oYXMtc29tZS1jcnlwdG8t=
Y29tcGxhaW50cz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwOTI5MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV=
0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjA5MjkmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3=
R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBffd36a27" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> =E2=80=9CHowey test=E2=
=80=9D of securities law</a> says that something is a security if it involv=
es=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cthe investment of money in a common enterprise with a reas=
onable expectation of profits to be derived from the efforts of others,=E2=
=80=9D and if you consistently=C2=A0say =E2=80=9Cthere=E2=80=99s no enterpr=
ise, there=E2=80=99ll be no profits, and we=E2=80=99ll make no efforts=E2=
=80=9D then you can argue that your token is not a security.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you then also hire someone to do a bunch of=
 spoofing and wash trading in your token, that=E2=80=99s=C2=A0<em>bad</em>,=
 and somebody (the Commodity Futures Trading Commission? prosecutors?) migh=
t go after you for=C2=A0<em>fraud</em>, but the=C2=A0<em>SEC=C2=A0</em>won=
=E2=80=99t go after you for=C2=A0<em>securities fraud=C2=A0</em>because the=
 fraud that you are doing is not to a security. If your model is that the S=
EC has way less of a sense of humor about these things than anyone else, th=
en that might be a good outcome.</p>

adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Do Kwon</h2>
</td>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In entirely related news, let=E2=80=99s check =
in with Do Kwon, the founder of the blown-up Terra blockchain and its token=
 Luna, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29212280.28=
8530/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9jcnlwdG8tZnVnaXRpdmUtZG8ta3dvbn=
MtZmlybS1hY2N1c2VzLWtvcmVhbi1wcm9zZWN1dG9ycy1vZi1vdmVycmVhY2gtMTE2NjQzOTQwO=
DI_bW9kPWxlYWRfZmVhdHVyZV9iZWxvd19hX3BvczE/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB256b9b1=
b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> in an undisclosed location</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mr. Kwon faces charges of violating South Kore=
a=E2=80=99s capital market law, according to the country=E2=80=99s Yonhap N=
ews Agency. But Terraform Labs argued that the law would only apply to Luna=
 if it were a security. If it isn=E2=80=99t a security, then Mr. Kwon and h=
is firm didn=E2=80=99t do anything illegal, the Terraform Labs spokesman sa=
id.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Terraform Labs=E2=80=99 argument that Luna isn=
=E2=80=99t a security stems from the murky regulatory status of cryptocurre=
ncies, which many countries around the world have struggled with, including=
 the United States. Officials, such as Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen, hav=
e cited the collapse of TerraUSD as evidence for the need for stronger cryp=
to regulation.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Terraform Labs spokesman suggested that So=
uth Korean prosecutors had expanded the definition of a security in respons=
e to public pressure over the collapse of Luna, which has since been rename=
d =E2=80=9CLuna Classic=E2=80=9D as Mr. Kwon has sought to launch a new ver=
sion of the cryptocurrency.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWe believe, as do most in industry, t=
hat Luna Classic is not, and has never been, a security, despite any change=
s in interpretation that Korean financial officials may have recently adopt=
ed,=E2=80=9D Terraform Labs=E2=80=99 spokesman said.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you thought that Luna was an investment in =
the collaborative effort to build the value of the Terra blockchain, then (=
1) it=E2=80=99s a security<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-7" =
style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wi=
dth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !importan=
t; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[7]</span>
</a> and (2) maybe you wanted to buy it, which is why Luna had a market cap=
italization in the tens of billions of dollars. If you thought that Luna wa=
s just, like, an electronic token with no investment thesis, then maybe it =
wasn=E2=80=99t a security =E2=80=94=C2=A0but why were you buying it?</p>
